title: From Self To Others
study: A Study In The Life Of Moses
scriptureForWeek: EXODUS 1 - EXODUS 2:10 EXODUS CHAPTER 1 These are the names of the sons of Israel (that is, Jacob) who moved to Egypt with their father, each with his family: Reuben, Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, Zebulun, Benjamin, Dan, Naphtali, Gad, and Asher. 5 In all, Jacob had seventy descendants in Egypt, including Joseph, who was already there. In time, Joseph and all of his brothers died, ending that entire generation. But their descendants, the Israelites, had many children and grandchildren. In fact, they multiplied so greatly that they became extremely powerful and filled the land. Eventually, a new king came to power in Egypt who knew nothing about Joseph or what he had done. He said to his people, “Look, the people of Israel now outnumber us and are stronger than we are. We must make a plan to keep them from growing even more. If we don’t, and if war breaks out, they will join our enemies and fight against us. Then they will escape from the country.” So the Egyptians made the Israelites their slaves. They appointed brutal slave drivers over them, hoping to wear them down with crushing labor. They forced them to build the cities of Pithom and Rameses as supply centers for the king. But the more the Egyptians oppressed them, the more the Israelites multiplied and spread, and the more alarmed the Egyptians became. So the Egyptians worked the people of Israel without mercy. They made their lives bitter, forcing them to mix mortar and make bricks and do all the work in the fields. They were ruthless in all their demands. Then Pharaoh, the king of Egypt, gave this order to the Hebrew midwives, Shiphrah and Puah: “When you help the Hebrew women as they give birth, watch as they deliver. If the baby is a boy, kill him; if it is a girl, let her live.” But
weekTheme: This week we launch into the second shift, From Self to Others. We are going to set the stage for the next 10 weeks by talking about how God has a plan and purpose for our lives. We are going to see that our lives are not accidents, that God is working in remarkable detail, and that he is doing this to prepare and use us to help others in incredible ways. Moses is one of the most inspiring people in the Bible and has so much to teach us about what it means to be a godly man, so let's lean in.

days.title: Born In A Time Of Trouble
days.scripture: EXODUS 1:1-14 1 These are the names of the sons of Israel (that is, Jacob) who moved to Egypt with their father, each with his family: 2 Reuben, Simeon, Levi, Judah, 3 Issachar, Zebulun, Benjamin, 4 Dan, Naphtali, Gad, and Asher. 5 In all, Jacob had seventy descendants in Egypt, including Joseph, who was already there. 6 In time, Joseph and all of his brothers died, ending that entire generation. 7 But their descendants, the Israelites, had many children and grandchildren. In fact, they multiplied so greatly that they became extremely powerful and filled the land. 8 Eventually, a new king came to power in Egypt who knew nothing about Joseph or what he had done. 9 He said to his people, “Look, the people of Israel now outnumber us and are stronger than we are. 10 We must make a plan to keep them from growing even more. If we don’t, and if war breaks out, they will join our enemies and fight against us. Then they will escape from the country.” 11 So the Egyptians made the Israelites their slaves. They appointed brutal slave drivers over them, hoping to wear them down with crushing labor. They forced them to build the cities of Pithom and Rameses as supply centers for the king. 12 But the more the Egyptians oppressed them, the more the Israelites multiplied and spread, and the more alarmed the Egyptians became. 13 So the Egyptians worked the people of Israel without mercy. 14 They made their lives bitter, forcing them to mix mortar and make bricks and do all the work in the fields. They were ruthless in all their demands.
days.reflection: Most stories start with once upon a time. They start in an ideal setting where everything is good until something goes wrong. This story isn’t one of those stories. This story starts with pain, heartache, and the murder of a generation of babies. But you should expect this now because you realize that life is hard and men’s stories are stories filled with difficulty. No matter how much a man does or how much he achieves, problems will always come back onto the scene. Look at how verse 8 of Exodus introduces the story of Moses to us. Eventually, a new king came to power in Egypt who knew nothing about Joseph or what he had done. Think back to all Joseph had done to help Pharaoh and the Egyptians. He interpreted their dreams, he managed their grain supply, ran the nation, and organized its salvation from starvation in a time of famine. Yet, none of this seems to matter to the leaders of the moment. They forgot Joseph and all that he had done. Because there was no memory, the Egyptians viewed the Israelites as a threat. They saw them growing in number and power and were worried that they would take over. So they oppressed them. Oppression is going to frame the story of Moses. Yes he will be a prince, yes he will be a deliver, yes he will be a friend of God, but first, he will be born into a broken world set on breaking others. All stories of rescue come out of disaster. All stories of hope come out of despair. All stories of peace come out of conflict. All stories of forgiveness come out of pain. We don’t get the good stuff without the bad stuff. We don’t get the freedom without the slavery. We don’t get prosperity without poverty. And we don’t have the option of living in a story without conflict, heartache, and pain. We are born into the middle of it. The Bible says that we are actually born into the middle of a spiritual war. There is a giant conflict. You are born in a time of trouble too. If you look around there is a ton of awful stuff happening around us. We went through a global pandemic, there are wars happening around the world, there is poverty, disease, and natural disasters. And there is stuff closer to home too. The betrayal of our friends, fights among family members, financial challenges, and sin staining almost everything we do. I know this all sounds kind of heavy and is a pretty intense way to start the second shift, but it's important to understand the nature of the world you live in, and the time of history you are born into. Get your mindset fixed on this reality and you will handle life well. Ignore it, and you won't be prepared to handle the things that come your way, and there will be lots of it. The good news is - God was with Moses and helped him walk through the challenges of evil with power and skill. And if you commit yourself to him, he will help you too.
questions: Why do you think it’s important to acknowledge the evil and brokenness around us? What happens if we don’t?
What sort of problems and fears do your friends tend to talk about? Are there any things that get you upset or concerned about?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share what life was like when they were born. What were the biggest issues and concerns back then? How did Christians respond to these challenges?

thoughts.verse: The LORD rescues the godly; he is their fortress in times of trouble. The LORD helps them, rescuing them from the wicked. He saves them, and they find shelter in him.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment and share the biggest problem you are facing with God right now. Invite him to help you in the thing that is causing you fear or concern.

days.title: Born For A Purpose
days.scripture: EXODUS 1:15-22 - 2:1-3 15 Then Pharaoh, the king of Egypt, gave this order to the Hebrew midwives, Shiphrah and Puah: 16 “When you help the Hebrew women as they give birth, watch as they deliver. If the baby is a boy, kill him; if it is a girl, let her live.” 17 But because the midwives feared God, they refused to obey the king’s orders. They allowed the boys to live, too. 18 So the king of Egypt called for the midwives. “Why have you done this?” he demanded. “Why have you allowed the boys to live?” 19 “The Hebrew women are not like the Egyptian women,” the midwives replied. “They are more vigorous and have their babies so quickly that we cannot get there in time.” 20 So God was good to the midwives, and the Israelites continued to multiply, growing more and more powerful. 21 And because the midwives feared God, he gave them families of their own. 22 Then Pharaoh gave this order to all his people: “Throw every newborn Hebrew boy into the Nile River. But you may let the girls live.” 2:1 About this time, a man and woman from the tribe of Levi got married. 2 The woman became pregnant and gave birth to a son. She saw that he was a special baby and kept him hidden for three months. 3 But when she could no longer hide him, she got a basket made of papyrus reeds and waterproofed it with tar and pitch. She put the baby in the basket and laid it among the reeds along the bank of the Nile River.
days.reflection: Being born is kind of crazy. Think about it. You had no control over showing up on this planet. You had no control over the family you would be born in, the time of history you’d live, the color of your skin, your gender, your nationality. You just showed up. Like a total surprise to everyone involved including you. You are here, and you don’t even remember how it happened. Wild, isn’t it? (By the way, we are all glad you are here.) But God has put us here right where he wants us. Acts 17:24-26 says, “He is the God who made the world and everything in it… He himself gives life and breath to everything, and he satisfies every need. From one man he created all the nations throughout the whole earth. He decided beforehand when they should rise and fall, and he determined their boundaries.” You were born for a purpose. The birth of a child is always a miracle, but sometimes there seems to be a little something extra about the birth of certain kids. Every now and then there is something about the baby that people can sense. A kind of destiny hovers over the child that makes people say, “that kid is gonna do something special someday.” This is what happened when Moses was born. Moses almost didn’t make it to the planet. Pharaoh, afraid of how quickly the Israelites were growing, put out a decree demanding that all Hebrew baby boys be drowned. They were to be thrown into the Nile river. Don’t gloss over that too quickly. Imagine police showing up at the hospital every time a boy was born in your area, and the police separated the crying baby from its screaming mother and threw him into the lake to drown. As barbaric as that sounds, that’s what happened. But God was looking out for Moses. His mother notices there was something special about him and so she hid him for 3 months. Then, instead of drowning him in the Nile, she gently placed him in a basket and entrusted him to God's care. Moses’ life was spared because God had a purpose for him. Pharaoh wasn’t in charge of his destiny, and even though the empire tried to kill him, God preserved his life. You are here for a purpose too. Your life is not an accident. God has specifically designed you with a unique call and mission too. Look at what God's word says about it in Psalm 139:13-18: "You made all the delicate, inner parts of my body and knit me together in my mother’s womb. Thank you for making me so wonderfully complex! Your workmanship is marvelous—how well I know it. You watched me as I was being formed in utter seclusion, as I was woven together in the dark of the womb. You saw me before I was born. Every day of my life was recorded in your book. Every moment was laid out before a single day had passed. How precious are your thoughts about me, O God. They cannot be numbered! I can’t even count them; they outnumber the grains of sand! And when I wake up, you are still with me!" The reason this is important to remember is that many young guys struggle with meaninglessness, anxiety, and depression. If you listen to the story of the culture, it will tell you that we are all accidents. We came here by accident, our lives have no clear plan, and when we die that’s it. We are just the random product of energy, matter, chance, and time. But for believers, we can live with confidence. We are born with a purpose; we are chosen and we are loved. We can live with confidence knowing God will reveal his plan as we trust in him. God wants you here. We want you here. Let's go!
questions: Do kids your age spend much time talking about the idea of their being “a purpose for your life?” If not, what do they talk about instead? If they do, what sort of stuff do they say about it?
Have you ever thought about the fact that there is a plan and purpose for your specific life? Have you ever gotten any clues as to what it may be?
Ask the person you are doing this study with if there are any cool stories from when you were a baby about any sort of near misses or cool escapes you had.

thoughts.verse: The LORD will work out his plans for my life— for your faithful love, O LORD, endures forever. Don’t abandon me, for you made me.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to thank God for giving you the gift of life and creating you with a purpose.

days.title: God Of The Details
days.scripture: EXODUS 2:5-6 5 Soon Pharaoh’s daughter came down to bathe in the river, and her attendants walked along the riverbank. When the princess saw the basket among the reeds, she sent her maid to get it for her. 6 When the princess opened it, she saw the baby. The little boy was crying, and she felt sorry for him. “This must be one of the Hebrew children,” she said.
days.reflection: Life is full of impossible odds. Some of them are just crazy. You are the 1 out of 400 trillion sperm that fertilized the egg to become you. Congratulations, you are a winner. 95 percent of the universe is invisible. We can’t even see the majority of what is happening around us. Your body is home to around 37 trillion cells. There is a lot going on inside of you that you probably never think about. There are approximately 108 billion people who have ever lived. You are one of them. Good to have you. The odds of winning the lottery are roughly one in 292.2 million. Open a savings account instead. Though we think life is filled with massive danger, your odds of dying of something kind of crazy are low. Plane crash: 11 million to 13 Car accident: 107 to 14 Falling out of bed: 2 million to 15 Flesh-eating bacteria: 1 million to 16 Lightning strike: 1.2 million to 17 Dog mauling: 118,776 to 14 Snakebite: 50 million to 18 Shark attack: 3.75 million to 19 It is crazy that swimming with sharks is safer than driving your car, sleeping in your bed, or even petting a dog. With so much statistical opposability for how things work, it’s actually a miracle that you are even here at all. It may be a numerical miracle, but it’s not an accident. God is working in the smallest details to bring about his plans. We can see this in the life of Moses. He is put into the Nile at the perfect moment for Pharaoh’s daughter to find him. This was highly improbable. He could have drifted by, she could have gone to a different part of the river, or his basket could have flipped over and he slowly drowned, yet God was working out his plan for Moses. Even when he was a powerless baby, with no ability to influence the outcome of his life, God was making sure his purpose for Moses prevailed. Proverbs 16:9 says, “We can make our plans, but the LORD determines our steps.” What seemed like tiny events were actually massive moments. Big doors open on small hinges. God is in the details. You can trust that God can work in your life to bring his purpose about for you. He wasn’t just in charge of Moses’ life, but he will work in yours if you let him. He can do things you cannot comprehend. He can take tiny details and turn them into keys that open doors to your future. The classes you take, the friends you make, the school you go to, the new kid at youth group, the people who move down the streets - these ordinary things may actually be extraordinary if you have eyes to see and are patient enough to wait for God to work. Don’t just look for big miracles and breakthroughs, pay attention to the details. Your heavenly Father sees you. Matthew 10:29-31 “What is the price of two sparrows —one copper coin? But not a single sparrow can fall to the ground without your Father knowing it. And the very hairs on your head are all numbered. So don’t be afraid; you are more valuable to God than a whole flock of sparrows." Fear not, it's gonna work out for good, eventually.
questions: Can you think of a scene in one of your favorite movies where a tiny little detail ended up making a big difference?
Can you think of a moment in your own life where a tiny little detail ended up making a massive difference?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share a story of how God used a small little detail in their life to do something really important later on.

thoughts.verse: Trust in the LORD with all your heart; do not depend on your own understanding. Seek his will in all you do, and he will show you which path to take.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to open your eyes to the wonder that is all around you. Ask him to highlight some of the small but cool things he may be doing in your life that you have previously overlooked.

days.title: Open Hearts, Open Doors
days.scripture: EXODUS 2:1-9 1 About this time, a man and woman from the tribe of Levi got married. 2 The woman became pregnant and gave birth to a son. She saw that he was a special baby and kept him hidden for three months. 3 But when she could no longer hide him, she got a basket made of papyrus reeds and waterproofed it with tar and pitch. She put the baby in the basket and laid it among the reeds along the bank of the Nile River. 4 The baby’s sister then stood at a distance, watching to see what would happen to him. 5 Soon Pharaoh’s daughter came down to bathe in the river, and her attendants walked along the riverbank. When the princess saw the basket among the reeds, she sent her maid to get it for her. 6 When the princess opened it, she saw the baby. The little boy was crying, and she felt sorry for him. “This must be one of the Hebrew children,” she said. 7 Then the baby’s sister approached the princess. “Should I go and find one of the Hebrew women to nurse the baby for you?” she asked. 8 “Yes, do!” the princess replied. 9 So the girl went and called the baby’s mother. “Take this baby and nurse him for me,” the princess told the baby’s mother. “I will pay you for your help.” So the woman took her baby home and nursed him.
days.reflection: Our world today talks a lot about luck. We think random good things happen to some people and random bad things happen to others. Maybe you have thought this to yourself - “Wow, I was lucky today”, or “Why do I have to be so unlucky” when things go wrong. But for Christians, our lives are not controlled by luck. If you recall from Joseph's life, our lives are directed by the providence of God. God’s providence is actually amazing. Providence is a big word with a really cool reality behind it. The word “providence” is from the word “provide” which has two parts: “pro” (Latin: “forward,” “on behalf of”) and “vide” (Latin: “to see”). It sounds like it would mean “to see forward” or “to foresee.” But it doesn’t. It means “to supply what is needed; to give sustenance or support.” The noun “providence” has come to mean God's actions of “providing for, sustaining, and governing the world.” God is intricately involved in guiding and directing things to make sure that his will and purposes come to pass. It shows that our lives are not left to random chance or blind luck, but to the direct love and care of God. Moses’ life has the providence of God all over it. Not just in the details of how he ended up in the hands of Pharaoh's daughter, but the things that happen after that. She is moved with compassion for him and decides to care for him, even though this is against the explicit command of her Father. His sister steps forward and offers to make sure the baby is cared for and nursed. She suggests Moses’ own mother as the one that should care for and nurse him. Pharaoh’s daughter then pays Moses’ mother to raise her own son. You can’t make a story like this up. It just shows, again and again, God working with the smallest possible details to get what he wants accomplished. He changes the attitudes and hearts of people; he organizes the times and locations of events; he works out good from horrible situations in difficult times. This should bring you a ton of comfort as you grow up and begin to move into manhood. Your growth may be hard, and you may face times of pressure, anxiety, fear and loneliness. But you can take heart because God is behind the scenes quietly working out the details of your story to work out his plan for your life. Proverbs 21:1 says, “The king’s heart is like a stream of water directed by the LORD; he guides it wherever he pleases.” God will direct your path, open closed doors, show you the way, and lead you to the things he has for you. But you have to learn to trust him. You’re not lucky or unlucky; you are loved and directed by God himself.
questions: Do you think God has the power to change people’s hearts? Why or why not? Does this violate their free will?
Have you ever felt God influencing your heart, or changing your perspective on something? If so, what was it?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share a time God changed their mind or heart on something. What was it, and how did he do it?

thoughts.verse: You can make many plans, but the LORD’s purpose will prevail.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you from it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to thank God that he has the power to accomplish his purpose for you. Ask him to continue to change your thoughts and desires to align with what he has for you.

days.title: Positioned For A Purpose
days.scripture: EXODUS 2:7-10 7 Then the baby’s sister approached the princess. “Should I go and find one of the Hebrew women to nurse the baby for you?” she asked. 8 “Yes, do!” the princess replied. So the girl went and called the baby’s mother. 9 “Take this baby and nurse him for me,” the princess told the baby’s mother. “I will pay you for your help.” So the woman took her baby home and nursed him. 10 Later, when the boy was older, his mother brought him back to Pharaoh’s daughter, who adopted him as her own son. The princess named him Moses, for she explained, “I lifted him out of the water.”
days.reflection: Where you grow up matters. It shapes a whole lot about how you view the world. It shapes your view of money; it shapes your view of sexuality; it shapes your view of God; it shapes your view of others. Are you aware of how your view of these things has been shaped by where you have grown up? God is going to position Moses to grow up in Pharaoh’s house. He is going to be raised as royalty, not as a slave. This is going to be absolutely essential for God's plan for him later in his life. Imagine how different Moses’ life could have been if he grew up in slavery. He would have worked to the point of exhaustion every day. He would have very limited options for his future. He would have had a tiring and meaningless job working to build another king's empire. He would have lived, worked, and then died in obscurity like the thousands of other Hebrew slaves whose names we do not know. But God wanted to use Moses as a deliverer, not a worker. He wanted to get him to a place to be able to do something about the conditions of his people. Though Moses is just a baby and doesn’t understand all this just yet, God is going to raise him in the very house that later on he will tear down. A pretty big calling for a tiny baby pulled from a river. Even though it must have been heartbreaking for Moses’ mother to send her son away, it was better than seeing him cruelly drowned as a baby. She could entrust him into God's hands and rest her heart knowing that she had done all she could to love the baby God had given her. She made the best decision she knew in the horrible and heartbreaking world she lived in. God will honor her bravery, and this baby will return to her in ways she couldn’t even dream of. Not just as a son, but as a leader, a prophet, and a man who walks with God. God put Moses at the perfect time in history, in the wildest circumstances, to work his purpose out for his people. God knew what he was doing, even if others couldn’t see it at the time. He was born in the midst of trouble, born for a purpose, directed by God's providence, shown incredible favor, and raised as a prince. God has put you at the right time of history for your life too! He has important things for you to do for his kingdom, and your family is an important part of that shaping process. That annoying sibling, those household chores, and those demanding parents could be the very thing you need to shape you into your calling and destiny. The place you grow up is important. It gives you a sense of identity and worth. It teaches you about wrong and right, how to succeed in life, and provides security and love. God has chosen the family that you are growing up in. He believed this was the perfect spot to help shape you into who he wants you to be. No family is perfect. They all have flaws and faults and sometimes can be embarrassing and weird. But you should feel pretty grateful for your home. You have a dad who is taking the time to help you become a man after all.
questions: Can you see a difference between your family and your friends’ families? What are a couple of differences that stand out to you?
If you could be raised in any family from television or the movies, which family would it be and why?

Ask the person doing this study with you what his family was like when he was your age. What were some of the good things, and what were some of the challenging things? How did it impact and shape him as a man?

thoughts.verse: My child, listen when your father corrects you. Don’t neglect your mother’s instruction. What you learn from them will crown you with grace and be a chain of honor around your neck.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to thank God for your family. Thank him for positioning you right where you are and for the unique purposes he is setting you up for.

extras: WEEK 1 MANSCHOOL BIRTHDAY BASH MANSCHOOL REMINDER The recipe for fun is pretty simple when you’re raising boys: add to any activity an element of danger, stir in a little exploration, add a dash of destruction, and you’ve got yourself a winner. - John Eldredge OPTION 1. ACTIVITY Take your son out for dinner at a nice spot. Then at the end of the meal, have the server bring out a cake with a candle and sing happy birthday. When he acts confused, let him know you simply wanted to celebrate that he is here. Remind him he is born for a reason and a purpose, and that it’s a joy to be his Dad. Let him know how much you love having him in your life. Then give him a birthday present (something he has talked about wanting for a while) to celebrate him. Let him know you are glad he was born, not just one day a year. OPTION 2. WATCH Watch Prince of Egypt This is a cartoon classic that gives a good reminder and overview of Moses’ life. If it’s a bit below his interest level, you may try Exodus: Gods and Kings by Ridley Scott. OPTION 3. DINNER AND DISCUSSION Recap some of the big ideas and see if he has any lingering questions. There was some pretty heady stuff this week. Provence, planning, God influencing tiny events, free will. See if he wants to go deep on any of this stuff.
FIELD NOTES PRAYER FOR MY SON
